literal: passion, attachment, coloring, dyeing; action-noun from √rañj 'to color, to be dyed, to be attached' (rañj + ghañ → rāga), hence the mind 'colored' by what it clings to
invariant_definition: *Rāga* is attachment in the sense of being 'colored' by what one clings to: the affective pull toward objects of pleasure that, on the traditional image, dyes the mind and biases its responses. The etymology from √rañj ('to dye, to color') informs the usage — the attached mind is said to take on the hue of its object as cloth takes a dye, the same root yielding *rāga* in its later musical sense of a 'coloring' melodic mode. *Rāga* is the attraction-pole of an affective pair, regularly coupled with *dveṣa* (aversion, repulsion); the two together form the attraction–repulsion polarity that the traditions take to structure conditioned experience, and freedom from both (*rāga-dveṣa-vimukta*) is a standard description of the disciplined. Several near-terms cluster around it: *saṅga* ('clinging, association'), the dwelling-on of which the *Gītā* makes the seed of desire (2.62); and *rasa*, the lingering 'taste' or relish for an object that can persist after its outward abandonment. The term has several analytic settings. In Pātañjala Yoga *rāga* is the third of the five *kleśas* (*sukhānuśayī rāgaḥ*, YS 2.7), the latent disposition of attachment-following-pleasure rooted in *avidyā*. In Buddhist usage *rāga* is one of the three poisons (*rāga, dveṣa, moha*), the greed whose extinction is identified with liberation. In Trika Śaivism *rāga* is not classed as an affliction but as one of the six *kañcukas*, a contraction of the self's fullness (*Paramārthasāra* 16–17). The term thus names a coloring attachment throughout, while its status — affliction, poison, or structural limitation — varies by system.

school_framing.shared_core: *Rāga* names **the affective attachment that 'colors' the mind toward objects of pleasure, the attraction-pole paired with *dveṣa* (aversion)**. The traditions treat this attraction–repulsion polarity as a primary source of disturbance and bondage. They differ on its precise classification (a standing *kleśa*, a root-poison, or a limiting *kañcuka*) and on what its removal accomplishes.
school_framing.register_axes_note: **Same concept, different aspect** between Yoga (one of the five *kleśas*) and Buddhism (one of the three poisons): both diagnose *rāga* as a defilement to be uprooted for liberation. **Real disagreement** with Trika, where *rāga* is not an affliction to be annihilated but one of the *kañcukas* — a real contraction of the self's fullness (*pūrṇatva*) — whose resolution is recognition (*pratyabhijñā*), not extinction.

per_school.definition: In Pātañjala Yoga *rāga* is the third of the five *kleśas*: *sukhānuśayī rāgaḥ*, attachment that dwells upon (follows from) pleasure (YS 2.7), paired with *dveṣa*, aversion dwelling upon pain (YS 2.8). Both are rooted in *avidyā* and are to be attenuated by *kriyā-yoga* and finally dissolved as discrimination matures, the standing counter-discipline being *vairāgya* (YS 1.15–16). *Rāga* is thus not a mere preference but a deep, latent disposition conditioning the *citta*, which must be uprooted for the *vṛttis* to be stilled.

In Buddhist analysis *rāga* (often *lobha*) is greed or lust, one of the three unwholesome roots and three fires (*rāga, dveṣa, moha*) whose going-out is *nibbāna*. As craving for sensual experience it drives the wheel of dependent origination; its removal is not a metaphysical recognition but the dispassion (*virāga*) cultivated through the path. *Virāga* — fading-away of attachment — is a standard synonym for the goal itself.
primary_loci: the three akusala-mūla / three fires (rāga, dveṣa, moha) in the suttas
Dhammapada (the rāga-vagga; virāga as the goal)
per_school.register_tag: Φ+S; reflective reader (rāga as poison; virāga as goal)

per_school.definition: In the Vedāntic-Gītā register *rāga* is the attachment whose absence marks the disciplined knower: he is *vīta-rāga-bhaya-krodhaḥ* (2.56) and acts with senses freed of *rāga* and *dveṣa* (2.64), thereby reaching serenity. Śaṅkara stresses the nuance of 2.59: outward abstention removes the objects but not the *rasa*, the inner relish; only the vision of the Supreme (*paraṃ dṛṣṭvā*) dissolves that lingering taste. Attachment is therefore not finally conquered by suppression but by knowledge that empties the object of its perceived value.

In Trika *rāga* is not primarily an affliction to be annihilated but one of the six *kañcukas*, the contractions through which the unlimited fullness (*pūrṇatva*) of Śiva is veiled in the bound subject (alongside *kalā, vidyā, kāla, niyati*). As a *kañcuka*, *rāga* names the limited attachment or partial interest that arises once the self's complete self-sufficiency is contracted — the felt need for *this* particular thing, in contrast to the all-fulfilment of the unbound self. Its resolution is therefore the re-expansion of consciousness in recognition (*pratyabhijñā*), not the mere extinction of desire.

textual_history.summary: *Rāga* is a post-Vedic term. Its root √rañj ('to dye, to colour, to redden') is Vedic and is the live image throughout — the attached mind 'coloured' by its object as cloth takes a dye — but the noun *rāga* in the sense of affective attachment is not a category of the early śruti; the Ṛgveda offers the colour/reddening sense of the root, not the psychological abstraction. The technical *rāga* belongs to the classical analyses: in Pātañjala Yoga it is the third of the five *kleśas*, attachment-following-pleasure (YS 2.3, 2.7); in the *Bhagavad-Gītā* it is the attraction-pole of the *rāga-dveṣa* pair whose mastery marks the *sthitaprajña* (2.56, 2.64). The Buddhist *rāga* (one of the three poisons) and its counter *virāga* ('fading of passion', a name for the goal) are the parallel development.

stages.locus: √rañj 'to dye/redden' — the root underlying rāga; the abstract noun is not yet a Vedic category
stages.sanskrit: (rañj → rāga: 'colouring, dyeing'; later 'the mind coloured by its object')
stages.rendering: to dye, to colour, to redden → 'a colouring' → attachment that dyes the mind
stages.note: Honest stage-marker, not a verse-quotation: *rāga* as 'attachment' is post-Vedic. What the Vedic layer supplies is only the root-image of √rañj, dyeing/colouring, which the classical term later turns into a psychology — the attached mind takes the hue of what it clings to, as the same root yields *rāga* in its musical sense of a 'colouring' melodic mode. We mark the term's earliest *real* attestation as classical (below) rather than force a Vedic stage.

stages.rendering: Ignorance, I-am-ness, attraction, aversion, and clinging-to-life are the afflictions. … Attraction is that which dwells upon pleasure.
stages.note: The earliest technical fixing in our corpus: *rāga* is the third *kleśa*, the latent disposition of attachment-following-pleasure, paired with *dveṣa* (2.8) and rooted in *avidyā*. Its standing counter-discipline is *vairāgya* (vi-rāga, 'de-colouring'; YS 1.15–16) — the negative directly built on it.

stages.sanskrit: duḥkheṣv anudvigna-manāḥ sukheṣu vigata-spṛhaḥ | vīta-rāga-bhaya-krodhaḥ sthita-dhīr munir ucyate
stages.rendering: Unshaken in mind amid sorrows, with hankering gone amid pleasures, free of attachment, fear, and anger (vīta-rāga-bhaya-krodha) — the sage of steady wisdom is so called.
stages.note: The Gītā uses *rāga* as the attachment whose absence marks the *sthitaprajña* (vīta-rāga, 2.56; rāga-dveṣa-viyukta, 2.64). Śaṅkara's nuance at 2.59: outward abstention removes the objects but not the *rasa*, the inner relish — 'the relish too turns away only on seeing the Supreme' (raso 'py asya paraṃ dṛṣṭvā nivartate) — so *rāga* is finally undone by knowledge, not suppression. (Trika's *rāga*-as-*kañcuka* is a still later, divergent re-use; see the per-school material.)

translator_note: 'Passion' is acceptable but can suggest mere intensity; the core of *rāga* is attachment that 'colors' the mind, the attraction-pole paired with *dveṣa* (aversion). Keep that polarity visible — rendering *rāga-dveṣa* as a single 'desire' loses the structure. Note the related cluster: *saṅga* ('clinging') and the residual *rasa* ('relish, lingering taste') are attachment-words near *rāga*, but *rasa* in particular carries the nuance of a taste that survives outward abstention. Above all, do not assume *rāga* is always an affliction: in Trika it is a *kañcuka*, a structural limitation to be recognized through, not a defilement to be extinguished, and the English should not pre-load the diagnostic frame.
